title: Chicken Biryani
description: A practical home-style Chicken Biryani recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tsp saffron soaked in 2 tbsp warm milk
steps: Rinse the basmati rice 3 to 4 times until the water is nearly clear, then soak it in cool water for 30 minutes and drain.
Heat the oil or ghee in a heavy pot over medium heat. Fry the sliced onions for 10 to 12 minutes until deep golden; remove half for layering.
Add the ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute. Add the chicken and sear over medium-high heat for 6 to 8 minutes, turning until lightly browned.
Lower to medium heat, add tomatoes, yogurt and ground spices, and cook uncovered for 12 to 15 minutes, stirring every 2 to 3 minutes, until the masala is thick and the oil begins to separate.
Add potatoes when listed and 1/2 to 1 cup water. Cover and simmer until the meat is tender and the gravy is thick; allow about 20 minutes for chicken or 45 to 60 minutes for beef or mutton.

Meanwhile, boil a large pot of well-salted water with optional whole spices. Add the drained rice and boil for 5 to 7 minutes, until the grains are about 70% cooked and still firm in the centre; drain immediately.
Spread the rice over the meat masala. Add the reserved fried onions, mint, coriander, green chillies, lemon and any saffron milk in even layers without stirring.

Cover tightly. Steam over medium heat for 5 minutes, then reduce to the lowest heat and cook for 20 minutes. Turn off the heat and rest, still covered, for 10 minutes before gently lifting the rice from the sides to mix.

title: Yakhni Pulao
description: A practical home-style Yakhni Pulao recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 750 g beef or mutton with bones
steps: Rinse the rice until the water is mostly clear, soak for 30 minutes, then drain.
Place the meat, water, ginger-garlic paste, whole spices and 1 teaspoon salt in a pot. Bring to a boil, skim the foam, then cover and simmer gently for 45 to 60 minutes until the meat is tender.
Strain the stock, discard the whole spices and measure 3 1/2 cups stock for 2 cups rice. Keep the meat and stock hot.
Heat oil or ghee over medium heat and fry the onion for 8 to 10 minutes until golden. Stir in the yogurt and cook for 2 minutes.
Add the cooked meat and sauté for 3 to 4 minutes. Pour in the measured hot stock, taste and adjust salt, then bring to a full boil.
Add the drained rice and boil uncovered over medium-high heat until the liquid falls just below the rice, about 6 to 8 minutes.
Cover tightly, reduce to the lowest heat and steam for 15 minutes. Rest covered for 10 minutes, then fluff gently with a fork.

title: Kabuli Pulao
description: A practical home-style Kabuli Pulao recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the rice well, soak for 30 minutes and drain.
Heat 2 tablespoons oil over medium-high heat. Brown the meat for 6 to 8 minutes, add the onion and cook until golden, then add 4 cups water, salt and pepper. Cover and simmer until tender.
Remove the meat and strain the stock. Measure 3 1/2 cups stock for the rice, adding water if necessary.
In a small pan, heat 1 tablespoon oil. Cook the carrots with sugar for 4 to 5 minutes, then add raisins and almonds and cook for 1 minute; set aside.
Bring the measured stock to a boil, add the drained rice and cook uncovered until most liquid is absorbed and the rice is about 80% tender.
Place the meat over the rice, sprinkle with cumin and cardamom, and cover tightly. Steam over very low heat for 18 to 20 minutes.
Rest for 10 minutes. Fluff the rice gently and arrange the sweet carrot, raisin and almond topping over the pulao before serving.

title: Chicken Pulao
description: A practical home-style Chicken Pulao recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the rice 3 to 4 times, soak for 20 to 30 minutes and drain.
Heat oil or ghee in a heavy pot over medium heat. Fry the onion for 8 to 10 minutes until evenly golden, then add the whole spices and stir for 30 seconds.
Add the listed meat or vegetables and sauté for 4 to 6 minutes. For meat pulao, add stock and simmer until the meat is nearly tender before adding rice.
Add the drained rice and the measured hot liquid. Use approximately 1 3/4 cups liquid per cup of soaked basmati rice, adjusting for any liquid already in the pot.
Season with salt, bring to a brisk boil and cook uncovered for 5 to 7 minutes, until the liquid level drops just below the rice.
Cover tightly, reduce to the lowest heat and steam for 15 minutes. Do not stir while the rice is steaming.
Turn off the heat, rest covered for 10 minutes, then fluff from the sides with a fork and serve.

title: Zarda Rice
description: A practical home-style Zarda Rice recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1/4 tsp yellow food colour or saffron
steps: Rinse the rice until the water is clear, soak for 30 minutes and drain.
Bring 4 cups water to a rolling boil. Add the rice and colour or saffron and cook for 6 to 8 minutes, until about 90% tender; drain carefully.
Heat ghee in a heavy pot over medium-low heat and bloom the cardamom for 30 seconds.
Add sugar with 1/2 cup water and simmer for 3 to 4 minutes, stirring until fully dissolved.
Fold in the drained rice with a wide spatula, taking care not to break the grains. Add raisins, nuts and candied fruit.
Cover tightly and steam over the lowest heat for 12 to 15 minutes, stirring once halfway through so the syrup coats evenly.
Rest covered for 5 minutes, then fluff gently and serve warm.

title: Chicken Karahi
description: A practical home-style Chicken Karahi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oil or ghee in a wide karahi over medium-high heat. Add the chicken and sear for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring frequently, until lightly browned.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute. Add tomatoes and salt, cover and cook over medium heat for 10 minutes until the tomatoes soften.
Remove the lid and mash the tomatoes into the pan juices. Add cumin, coriander, chilli and any listed white pepper or yogurt.
Cook uncovered over medium-high heat for 10 to 15 minutes, stirring and scraping the bottom every 1 to 2 minutes, until the sauce clings to the meat and the oil separates.
For mutton, add 1 to 1 1/2 cups water after searing, cover and simmer for 35 to 50 minutes before reducing the sauce. For chicken, add only a small splash if needed.
Add crushed black pepper, green chillies and garam masala. Cook for 2 minutes; chicken must reach 74°C (165°F) internally.
Turn off the heat, add fresh coriander and rest for 5 minutes before serving.

licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.

title: White Karahi
description: A practical home-style White Karahi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 750 g boneless chicken, cut into pieces
steps: Whisk yogurt, cream, cumin, coriander, white pepper, half the black pepper and salt for 1 minute until smooth.
Heat oil or ghee in a wide karahi over medium-high heat. Add chicken and sear for 6 to 8 minutes until lightly browned.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute. Lower the heat and add the yogurt mixture gradually while stirring continuously.
Cover and cook over medium-low heat for 12 to 15 minutes, stirring every 3 minutes, until the chicken reaches 74°C (165°F).
Uncover and cook over medium-high heat for 5 to 7 minutes until the sauce thickens and the oil begins to separate.
Add green chillies, remaining black pepper and garam masala. Cook for 2 minutes, then garnish with ginger and coriander.

title: Peshawari Karahi
description: A practical home-style Peshawari Karahi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: Fresh ginger and coriander for garnish
steps: Heat oil or ghee in a wide karahi over medium-high heat. Add the chicken or mutton and sear for 7 to 9 minutes until browned.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute, then add tomatoes, cumin and salt.
Cover and cook over medium heat until the tomatoes collapse—10 minutes for chicken or 15 minutes for mutton.
Mash the tomatoes into the meat and add water only if the pan is dry. For mutton, cover and simmer for 35 to 50 minutes until tender.
Uncover, add crushed black pepper and green chillies and cook over high heat for 7 to 10 minutes, stirring often, until the masala clings to the meat.
Chicken must reach 74°C (165°F). Finish with ginger and coriander and rest for 5 minutes.

title: Chicken Korma
description: A practical home-style Chicken Korma recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil or ghee in a heavy pot over medium heat. Fry the sliced onions for 10 to 12 minutes until deep golden; remove, cool slightly and crush half.
Add whole spices to the same fat and stir for 30 seconds. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add the chicken and sear over medium-high heat for 6 to 8 minutes until the surface loses its raw colour.
Lower the heat. Add the whisked yogurt 2 tablespoons at a time, stirring continuously after each addition so it does not split.
Add ground spices, crushed fried onion and water. Cover and simmer gently until tender—20 to 25 minutes for chicken or 45 to 60 minutes for beef or mutton.
Uncover and cook over medium heat for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring often, until the gravy is glossy and the oil separates. Add small splashes of hot water only if the masala catches.
Finish with garam masala, adjust salt and rest covered for 5 minutes before serving.

licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.

title: Nihari
description: A practical home-style Nihari recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 kg beef shank or boneless stew meat
3 tbsp whole-wheat flour mixed with 1/2 cup water
Julienned ginger, coriander, green chillies and lemon for serving
steps: Heat oil or ghee in a heavy pot over medium-high heat. Add the beef and sear for 8 to 10 minutes until browned on several sides.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute. Sprinkle in nihari masala, chilli powder, turmeric and salt; stir for 30 seconds without scorching.
Pour in the water or stock, bring to a boil, then cover and cook over very low heat for 2 1/2 to 3 1/2 hours, until the meat is spoon-tender. A pressure cooker may be used for 45 to 55 minutes.
Whisk the flour with 1/2 cup cool water until completely smooth. Slowly pour it into the simmering gravy while stirring continuously.
Simmer uncovered over low heat for 12 to 15 minutes, stirring every few minutes, until the gravy lightly coats a spoon.
Taste and adjust salt and chilli. Rest for 10 minutes so excess oil rises naturally to the surface.
Serve very hot with ginger, coriander, green chillies and lemon. Reheat leftovers to at least 74°C (165°F).

title: Paya
description: A practical home-style Paya recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 4 cleaned goat or beef trotters, cut into pieces
Ginger, coriander, green chilli and lemon for garnish
steps: Rinse the cleaned trotters or head-and-foot pieces thoroughly. Blanch in boiling water for 8 minutes, drain and rinse again to remove impurities.
Heat oil or ghee over medium heat and fry the onion for 8 to 10 minutes until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add yogurt and spices gradually, stirring for 4 to 5 minutes until smooth and glossy.

Add the blanched pieces and water. Bring to a boil, skim the surface, then cover and simmer on the lowest heat for 4 to 6 hours, or pressure-cook for 60 to 75 minutes, until the gelatin releases and the meat is tender.
Remove any loose bones, then simmer uncovered for 15 to 20 minutes to reach a lightly thick, silky consistency.
Adjust salt and garam masala. Keep the soup above 74°C (165°F) when serving.
Garnish with ginger, coriander, green chilli and lemon, and serve with naan.

title: Haleem
description: A practical home-style Haleem recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: Fried onions, ginger, coriander, green chilli and lemon for garnish
steps: Rinse the soaked wheat, barley and lentils. Place them in a large pot with 5 cups water and simmer over medium-low heat for 60 to 75 minutes, stirring every 10 minutes, until very soft.
In a separate heavy pot, heat oil over medium heat and fry the onions until deep golden. Reserve one-third for garnish.
Add ginger-garlic paste, meat, haleem masala, chilli and turmeric. Sear for 8 minutes, then add 2 cups water, cover and simmer until the meat is very tender.
Shred the cooked meat finely. Blend or mash the grain mixture with an immersion blender for 2 to 3 minutes until thick but not completely smooth.
Combine the meat and grain mixture. Cook over low heat for 30 to 40 minutes, stirring from the bottom every 3 to 4 minutes to prevent sticking.
Beat the haleem vigorously with a sturdy spoon or hand whisk for 5 to 7 minutes to develop the traditional stretchy, cohesive texture. Add hot water in small amounts if it becomes too thick.
Adjust salt and serve at least 74°C (165°F), topped with fried onions, ginger, coriander, green chilli and lemon.

title: Aloo Gosht
description: A practical home-style Aloo Gosht recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oil or ghee in a heavy pan over medium heat for 1 minute. Add onion when listed and cook for 7 to 9 minutes until soft and golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and stir for 45 to 60 seconds. Add the meat or mince and cook over medium-high heat for 6 to 8 minutes, breaking up mince with a spoon.
Add tomatoes, yogurt and ground spices. Cook over medium heat for 8 to 12 minutes, stirring every 1 to 2 minutes, until the masala thickens and looks glossy.
Add the listed potatoes, peas, chickpeas, lentils or greens according to their cooking time, followed by the measured water or stock.
Cover and simmer gently until everything is tender: about 20 minutes for boneless chicken, 30 minutes for mince, or 45 to 60 minutes for beef or mutton. Stir from the bottom every 8 to 10 minutes.
Uncover and reduce the sauce for 5 to 10 minutes. Add cream, butter, pickle spice or finishing herbs only after the meat, poultry or vegetables are fully cooked; chicken must reach 74°C (165°F).
Taste, adjust salt and acidity, and rest covered for 5 minutes before serving.

title: Kofta Curry
description: A practical home-style Kofta Curry recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 small onion, grated and squeezed dry
steps: Combine the mince, grated onion, gram flour, half the ginger-garlic paste, green chilli, coriander, half the garam masala and salt. Knead by hand for 3 to 4 minutes until slightly sticky.
With lightly oiled hands, shape into 16 equal balls. Chill for 20 minutes so they hold their shape.
Heat oil in a wide pot over medium heat. Fry the sliced onions for 8 to 10 minutes until golden, then add the remaining ginger-garlic paste for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes, yogurt and ground spices. Cook over medium heat for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring often, until the masala thickens and the oil separates.
Add water and bring to a gentle simmer. Lower the koftas into the gravy in one layer; do not stir for the first 8 minutes.
Cover and simmer gently for 18 to 22 minutes, shaking the pan occasionally, until the koftas reach at least 71°C (160°F) internally.
Uncover and reduce the gravy for 5 minutes if needed. Finish with garam masala and coriander and rest for 5 minutes before serving.

title: Mutton Rogan Josh
description: A practical home-style Mutton Rogan Josh recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil or ghee in a heavy pot over medium heat. Fry the sliced onions for 10 to 12 minutes until deep golden; remove, cool slightly and crush half.
Add whole spices to the same fat and stir for 30 seconds. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add the meat and sear over medium-high heat for 6 to 8 minutes until the surface loses its raw colour.
Lower the heat. Add the whisked yogurt 2 tablespoons at a time, stirring continuously after each addition so it does not split.
Add ground spices, crushed fried onion and water. Cover and simmer gently until tender—20 to 25 minutes for chicken or 45 to 60 minutes for beef or mutton.
Uncover and cook over medium heat for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring often, until the gravy is glossy and the oil separates. Add small splashes of hot water only if the masala catches.
Finish with garam masala, adjust salt and rest covered for 5 minutes before serving.

title: Chicken Tikka
description: A practical home-style Chicken Tikka recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tsp paprika or red chilli powder
steps: Whisk the yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, oil, salt and spices for 1 minute until smooth.
Add the chicken and turn until every piece is evenly coated. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours; tougher beef or mutton cuts may marinate for 6 to 8 hours.
Preheat a grill, barbecue or oven broiler to high, about 220°C (425°F). If using wooden skewers, soak them in water for 30 minutes.
Thread the pieces on skewers or arrange them on an oiled rack with space between each piece. Brush lightly with oil.
Cook for 12 to 20 minutes depending on size, turning every 4 to 5 minutes and basting once, until browned at the edges.
Check the thickest piece for doneness; it should reach 74°C (165°F).
Rest for 5 minutes, then serve with lemon, sliced onion and chutney.

title: Reshmi Kebab
description: A practical home-style Reshmi Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 small onion, grated and squeezed dry
steps: Combine chicken mince, onion, cream, yogurt, cashews, ginger-garlic paste, chilli, white pepper, garam masala, gram flour, coriander and salt.
Knead the mixture by hand for 4 to 5 minutes until smooth and sticky. Cover and refrigerate for 1 hour.
Divide into 10 portions and shape each around a lightly oiled skewer, pressing firmly so no cracks remain.
Preheat a grill or broiler to 220°C (425°F) and brush the kebabs lightly with oil.
Cook for 12 to 15 minutes, turning every 3 to 4 minutes, until lightly browned and the centre reaches 74°C (165°F).
Rest for 3 minutes and serve with lemon and chutney.

title: Chapli Kebab
description: A practical home-style Chapli Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 onion, finely chopped and squeezed dry
steps: Combine the mince with onion, tomato, egg, cornmeal, spices, chilli, coriander and salt. Mix by hand for 3 minutes without overworking.
Cover and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Divide into 8 equal portions and flatten each into a thin 12 cm patty.
Heat 5 mm oil in a heavy skillet over medium-high heat to about 175°C (350°F).
Fry the patties in batches for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deeply browned and the centre reaches 71°C (160°F). Do not crowd the pan.
Drain on a rack rather than paper so the crust remains crisp.
Rest for 3 minutes and serve hot with naan, sliced onion, tomato and chutney.

title: Shami Kebab
description: A practical home-style Shami Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Combine meat, soaked chana dal, onion, ginger-garlic paste, whole spices, salt and 2 cups water in a pot. Simmer uncovered for 35 to 45 minutes until tender and completely dry.
Cool for 15 minutes, remove the cinnamon and grind the mixture to a smooth, firm paste.
Mix in egg, coriander, mint and green chilli. Knead for 2 minutes, then chill for 20 minutes.
Shape into 12 flat patties. If the mixture feels soft, mix in 1 tablespoon gram flour.
Heat a thin layer of oil over medium heat and shallow-fry for 3 to 4 minutes per side until evenly browned and heated through to 74°C (165°F).
Drain briefly and serve hot with mint chutney and lemon.

title: Gola Kebab
description: A practical home-style Gola Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix the mince, fried onion, papaya paste, ginger-garlic paste, gram flour, yogurt, spices, coriander and salt.
Knead by hand for 5 minutes until very smooth and sticky, then refrigerate for 1 hour.
With oiled hands, shape into 12 compact oval kebabs or wrap portions around skewers.
Heat a grill, broiler or heavy skillet to high, about 220°C (425°F), and brush with oil.
Cook for 10 to 14 minutes, turning frequently, until evenly browned and the centre reaches 71°C (160°F).
Rest for 3 minutes before serving.

title: Bihari Kebab
description: A practical home-style Bihari Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk yogurt, papaya paste, fried onion paste, ginger-garlic paste, mustard oil, lemon, spices and salt for 1 minute.
Add the beef strips and massage the marinade into the meat for 2 minutes. Cover and refrigerate for 6 to 8 hours.
Thread the strips closely onto skewers and brush lightly with oil.
Grill or broil at 230°C (445°F) for 10 to 14 minutes, turning every 3 minutes, until charred at the edges and tender.
Rest for 5 minutes and serve with onion, lemon and chutney.

title: Dhaga Kebab
description: A practical home-style Dhaga Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Combine mince, onion, papaya, gram flour, ginger-garlic paste, spices, coriander and salt and knead for 5 minutes until sticky.
Chill for 1 hour, then shape the mixture into long kebabs around skewers.
Wrap clean cooking thread loosely around each kebab from end to end so it holds its shape.
Grill or broil at 220°C (425°F) for 12 to 15 minutes, turning every 3 minutes and brushing lightly with oil.
Cook until the centre reaches 71°C (160°F), then remove the thread carefully.
Rest for 3 minutes and serve hot.

licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.

title: Anda Shami Burger
description: A practical home-style Anda Shami Burger recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Prepare the filling completely before assembling. Cook meat over medium-high heat until browned and safely done, or fry the patties until crisp and hot in the centre.
Warm the bread, bun or paratha on a dry griddle over medium heat for 30 to 60 seconds per side.
Spread the listed chutney, sauce or dressing evenly, leaving a 1 cm border around the edge.
Arrange the hot filling in an even layer, followed by onion, tomato, pickles, cheese or herbs as listed.
For rolls, fold the lower edge over the filling and roll tightly. For pressed sandwiches, cook at medium heat for 2 to 3 minutes per side until the cheese melts.
Serve immediately while the bread is warm and the filling remains hot.

licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Prepare all cooked components first and cool them to room temperature. Keep crisp items in an airtight container until the final minute.

title: Dahi Bhallay
description: A practical home-style Dahi Bhallay recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 12 prepared lentil dumplings, soaked and squeezed
steps: Soak the lentils for 4 hours, drain well and grind with only 2 to 4 tablespoons cold water until thick and fluffy.
Beat the batter by hand for 2 minutes to incorporate air, then mix in salt and any listed spices.
Heat oil to 175°C (350°F). Fry tablespoon portions for 4 to 5 minutes, turning until evenly golden, then drain.
Soak the hot bhallay in warm water for 15 minutes. Gently press each between the palms to remove excess water without breaking it.
Whisk the yogurt for 1 minute with salt and a little sugar or water until smooth. Arrange the bhallay in a serving dish and cover with yogurt.
Spoon over tamarind and green chutneys, then sprinkle with chaat masala, roasted cumin and chilli.
Chill for 30 minutes and garnish just before serving so the bhallay remain soft and the toppings stay fresh.

title: Gol Gappay

licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Blend mint, coriander, green chilli, ginger, lemon, roasted cumin, chaat masala and black salt with 1 cup water for 60 seconds.
Strain into a jug, add the remaining chilled water and whisk for 30 seconds. Chill for at least 30 minutes.
Mix boiled potato and chickpeas with a little chaat masala, cumin and salt.
Keep the puri shells, filling, tamarind chutney and chilled spiced water in separate bowls until serving.
Crack a small opening in one puri, add 1 to 2 teaspoons filling and 1/2 teaspoon tamarind chutney.
Fill with the chilled water and eat immediately; assemble only one at a time so the shell remains crisp.

title: Saag
description: A practical home-style Saag recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 750 g spinach, mustard greens or mixed leafy greens
steps: Wash and dry the vegetables thoroughly, then cut them into even pieces so they cook at the same rate.
Heat oil in a wide pan over medium-high heat. Add whole spices or aromatics and stir for 30 to 45 seconds until fragrant.
Add firm vegetables first and sauté for 4 to 6 minutes. Add quick-cooking leafy vegetables later so they retain colour and texture.
Add tomatoes, sauce ingredients and ground spices. Cook over medium heat for 5 to 8 minutes, stirring every minute.
Add only the measured water or stock, cover and cook until just tender. Avoid overcooking; most vegetables need 8 to 15 minutes.
Uncover and cook over high heat for 2 to 4 minutes to remove excess moisture and concentrate the sauce.
Finish with lemon, herbs, sesame oil, cream or garam masala as listed and serve immediately.

title: Makki Ki Roti
description: A practical home-style Makki Ki Roti recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 2 tbsp ghee or butter for cooking
steps: Mix maize flour, whole-wheat flour and salt. Add warm water gradually and knead gently for 3 to 4 minutes into a soft dough.
Cover and rest for 10 minutes so the flour hydrates.
Divide into 6 balls. Pat one ball between damp palms or between two sheets of parchment into a 14 cm circle.
Heat a griddle over medium heat. Transfer the roti carefully and cook for 2 to 3 minutes until the edges look dry.
Flip, brush lightly with ghee and cook for another 2 minutes, pressing gently until brown spots appear.
Serve immediately with saag and butter.

title: Chana Masala
description: A practical home-style Chana Masala recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the lentils, beans or chickpeas thoroughly. Soak only when listed, then drain.

Place them in a pot with the measured water and turmeric. Bring to a boil, skim any foam, then simmer gently until tender—20 to 30 minutes for split lentils or 60 to 90 minutes for soaked whole beans.
In a separate pan, heat oil or ghee over medium heat. Cook onion for 7 to 9 minutes until golden, then add ginger-garlic paste for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes and ground spices and cook for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring often, until the masala thickens and the oil separates.
Add the cooked pulses with enough cooking liquid to reach the desired consistency. Simmer uncovered for 10 to 15 minutes.
Mash about one-quarter of the pulses against the side of the pot to thicken naturally. Adjust salt and acidity.
Prepare any final tempering in hot ghee for 30 to 45 seconds and pour it over the dish just before serving.

title: Kadhi Pakora
description: A practical home-style Kadhi Pakora recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk the yogurt, gram flour, turmeric, chilli powder, salt and 4 cups water for 2 minutes until completely smooth and free of lumps.
Pour into a heavy pot and bring to a gentle boil over medium heat, whisking continuously for the first 5 minutes so the yogurt does not split.
Reduce to low heat and simmer uncovered for 30 to 35 minutes, stirring every 4 to 5 minutes, until the kadhi is lightly thickened.
For the pakoras, mix gram flour, sliced onion, cumin, salt, baking soda and enough water to make a thick dropping batter. Rest for 10 minutes.
Heat oil to 175°C (350°F). Drop tablespoon portions of batter into the oil and fry for 4 to 5 minutes until golden and cooked through; drain.
Add the pakoras to the simmering kadhi and cook for 8 to 10 minutes so they soften slightly.
Heat the tempering oil, fry cumin, curry leaves and dried chillies for 30 seconds, then pour over the kadhi and serve hot.

title: Murgh Cholay
description: A practical home-style Murgh Cholay recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oil or ghee in a heavy pan over medium heat for 1 minute. Add onion when listed and cook for 7 to 9 minutes until soft and golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and stir for 45 to 60 seconds. Add the meat or mince and cook over medium-high heat for 6 to 8 minutes, breaking up mince with a spoon.
Add tomatoes, yogurt and ground spices. Cook over medium heat for 8 to 12 minutes, stirring every 1 to 2 minutes, until the masala thickens and looks glossy.
Add the listed potatoes, peas, chickpeas, lentils or greens according to their cooking time, followed by the measured water or stock.
Cover and simmer gently until everything is tender: about 20 minutes for boneless chicken, 30 minutes for mince, or 45 to 60 minutes for beef or mutton. Stir from the bottom every 8 to 10 minutes.
Uncover and reduce the sauce for 5 to 10 minutes. Add cream, butter, pickle spice or finishing herbs only after the meat, poultry or vegetables are fully cooked; chicken must reach 74°C (165°F).
Taste, adjust salt and acidity, and rest covered for 5 minutes before serving.

title: Sajji
description: A practical home-style Sajji recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 2 cups cooked rice for optional stuffing
steps: Pat the chicken dry inside and out. Rub with lemon juice, salt, black pepper, cumin, coriander and oil, including inside the cavity.
Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, then let stand at room temperature for 30 minutes before cooking.
Preheat the oven or covered grill to 190°C (375°F). Stuff loosely with cooked rice when using and tie the legs together.
Roast breast-side up for 60 to 80 minutes, rotating once and brushing with pan juices halfway through.
The thickest part of the thigh must reach 74°C (165°F) without touching bone.
Rest for 15 minutes before carving.

title: Kheer
description: A practical home-style Kheer recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1/2 cup basmati rice, washed and soaked 20 minutes
steps: Rinse and soak the rice when listed. Heat the milk in a heavy, wide pan over medium heat until steaming, stirring every 2 minutes.
Reduce to low heat, add rice or ground rice and cardamom, and simmer gently for 35 to 50 minutes. Scrape the base and sides every 3 to 4 minutes to prevent scorching.
When the grains are completely soft and the milk has reduced, add sugar and stir until dissolved.
Continue cooking for 8 to 12 minutes until the dessert coats the back of a spoon. Add cornstarch slurry only when listed and simmer for 2 minutes.
Stir in rose water, saffron or nuts after turning off the heat.
Serve warm, or cool to room temperature within 1 hour and refrigerate for at least 3 hours before serving chilled.

licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.

title: Sheer Khurma

licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat ghee in a heavy pan over medium-low heat and fry the vermicelli for 3 to 4 minutes until lightly golden.
Add milk and cardamom and bring to a gentle simmer, stirring every 1 to 2 minutes.
Cook for 8 to 12 minutes until the vermicelli is tender. Add sugar and stir until dissolved.
Add dates, raisins and nuts when listed and simmer for another 5 minutes.
Turn off the heat, add rose water and rest for 10 minutes. Serve warm or chill below 5°C (41°F).

title: Seviyan
description: A practical home-style Seviyan recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.

title: Zarda
description: A practical home-style Zarda recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1/4 tsp yellow food colour or saffron
steps: Rinse the rice until clear, soak for 30 minutes and drain.
Boil in coloured or saffron water for 6 to 8 minutes until about 90% tender, then drain.
Heat ghee in a heavy pot and fry cardamom for 30 seconds. Add sugar and 1/2 cup water and simmer for 3 minutes.
Fold in the rice gently with nuts, raisins and candied fruit. Add small sweets only during the final steaming stage.
Cover tightly and steam on the lowest heat for 12 to 15 minutes.
Rest covered for 5 minutes, fluff gently and serve warm.

title: Shahi Tukray
description: A practical home-style Shahi Tukray recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Cut the bread into triangles and shallow-fry in ghee over medium heat for 1 to 2 minutes per side until crisp and golden; drain.
Simmer the milk with cardamom over low heat for 25 to 35 minutes, stirring often, until reduced by about one-third.
Add sugar and saffron and simmer for 5 minutes, then add rose water.
Arrange the fried bread in a shallow dish and pour the hot reduced milk evenly over it.
Rest for 20 minutes so the bread absorbs the milk without completely losing its shape.
Garnish with nuts and serve warm or chilled.

title: Gajar Ka Halwa
description: A practical home-style Gajar Ka Halwa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Measure all ingredients before starting and warm the milk, water or syrup separately.

title: Ras Malai
description: A practical home-style Ras Malai recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Curdle the hot milk with lemon juice, strain through cloth and rinse the chenna under cold water.
Hang for 30 minutes, then knead the chenna by hand for 8 to 10 minutes until completely smooth and slightly greasy.
Shape into equal flat discs. Boil sugar and water in a wide pot, add the discs, cover and cook at a steady boil for 12 minutes.
Cool for 10 minutes, then gently press out excess syrup.
Simmer the serving milk with sugar, cardamom and saffron for 15 to 20 minutes, stirring often.
Add the discs, chill for at least 4 hours and garnish with pistachios.

title: Gulab Jamun
description: A practical home-style Gulab Jamun recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Prepare the syrup first. Boil sugar and water for 5 to 7 minutes, then add cardamom, lemon or rose water and keep warm at about 70°C (160°F).
Mix or knead the dough or batter only until smooth. For milk-solid doughs, knead gently for 2 to 3 minutes; for fermented batters, rest as directed until bubbly.
Shape equal portions or transfer batter to a piping bag. Keep pieces small enough to cook through evenly.
Heat oil or ghee to 150 to 160°C (300 to 320°F) for milk sweets, or 175°C (350°F) for flour-based fritters.
Fry in small batches, turning frequently, until evenly coloured and cooked through. Adjust the heat so the exterior does not darken before the centre cooks.
Drain for 20 seconds, then place directly into warm syrup. Soak for 20 minutes to 2 hours depending on the sweet.
Serve warm or at room temperature, and refrigerate milk-based sweets within 2 hours.

title: Rabri
description: A practical home-style Rabri recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse and soak the rice when listed. Heat the milk in a heavy, wide pan over medium heat until steaming, stirring every 2 minutes.
Reduce to low heat, add rice or ground rice and cardamom, and simmer gently for 35 to 50 minutes. Scrape the base and sides every 3 to 4 minutes to prevent scorching.
When the grains are completely soft and the milk has reduced, add sugar and stir until dissolved.
Continue cooking for 8 to 12 minutes until the dessert coats the back of a spoon. Add cornstarch slurry only when listed and simmer for 2 minutes.
Stir in rose water, saffron or nuts after turning off the heat.
Serve warm, or cool to room temperature within 1 hour and refrigerate for at least 3 hours before serving chilled.

title: Kulfi
description: A practical home-style Kulfi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Chill the moulds or freezer container. Combine the milk, cream and half the sugar in a heavy saucepan.
Heat over medium-low heat to 82°C (180°F), stirring every 30 seconds; do not allow it to boil over.
For egg-based mixtures, whisk yolks with the remaining sugar for 2 minutes until pale. Slowly whisk in 1 cup hot milk, then return everything to the pan.
Cook while stirring continuously until the base reaches 82 to 84°C (180 to 183°F) and lightly coats a spoon. For cornstarch or salep versions, simmer gently for 2 minutes.
Strain, cool over an ice bath and refrigerate for at least 4 hours. Add flavouring, nuts or fruit only after the base is cold.
Churn according to the machine instructions for 20 to 30 minutes, or freeze in moulds for 6 to 8 hours. For unchurned mixtures, whisk vigorously every 45 minutes for the first 3 hours.
Let stand at room temperature for 5 minutes before scooping.

licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Prepare and measure all components before assembly. Chill the serving dish or glasses when the dessert is served cold.

steps: Dry-roast sesame seeds over medium-low heat for 3 to 4 minutes and keep warm.

title: Bakarkhani
description: A practical home-style Bakarkhani recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Combine flour, yeast or leavening, sugar and salt. Add the wet ingredients and mix with a spoon until a rough dough forms.
Knead by hand for 8 to 10 minutes, or with a stand mixer fitted with a dough hook for 6 minutes on medium-low speed, until smooth and elastic.
Cover and let rise in a warm place for 60 to 90 minutes, until doubled. Unleavened doughs need only a 20 to 30 minute rest.
Divide evenly, shape and add any filling or topping. Rest the shaped dough for 10 to 20 minutes while heating the cooking surface.
For oven-baked naan or enriched breads, preheat to 230°C (445°F) and bake for 6 to 12 minutes until puffed and browned. For griddle breads, cook over medium-high heat for 1 to 2 minutes per side.
Brush with butter or ghee immediately after cooking and keep covered with a clean cloth until serving.

Divide evenly, shape and add any filling or topping. Rest the shaped dough for 10 to 20 minutes while heating the cooking surface.
For oven-baked naan or enriched breads, preheat to 230°C (445°F) and bake for 6 to 12 minutes until puffed and browned. For griddle breads, cook over medium-high heat for 1 to 2 minutes per side.
Brush with butter or ghee immediately after cooking and keep covered with a clean cloth until serving.

title: Tandoori Naan

licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.

title: Rusks
description: A practical home-style Rusks recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Preheat the oven to 175°C (350°F). Grease and line a 23 cm loaf pan.
Beat butter and sugar on medium speed for 3 minutes until light. Add eggs one at a time, beating 30 seconds after each.
Whisk flour, baking powder and salt separately. Add to the butter mixture in 3 additions, alternating with milk, and mix on low only until combined.
Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool completely, then slice into 1.5 cm pieces.
Arrange slices on trays and bake at 140°C (285°F) for 25 to 35 minutes, turning once, until dry and crisp.
Cool completely before storing.

title: Cake Rusk
description: A practical home-style Cake Rusk recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.

title: Nan Khatai
description: A practical home-style Nan Khatai recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Preheat the oven to 165°C (330°F) and line two trays.
Beat softened ghee and powdered sugar on medium speed for 3 minutes until pale and creamy.
Whisk the flours, baking powder and cardamom, then add to the ghee mixture and mix on low for 30 to 45 seconds until a soft dough forms.
Divide into 20 balls, flatten slightly and press pistachio on top.
Bake for 14 to 18 minutes until the edges are lightly golden; the centres will firm as they cool.
Cool on the tray for 10 minutes, then transfer to a rack.

title: Butter Biscuits
description: A practical home-style Butter Biscuits recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Preheat the oven to 175°C (350°F) and line baking trays.
Beat butter and powdered sugar on medium speed for 3 to 4 minutes until pale and fluffy.
Beat in the egg and vanilla for 30 seconds. Add flour, baking powder and salt and mix on low only until combined.
Pipe or shape equal biscuits and chill the trays for 15 minutes.
Bake for 10 to 14 minutes until the edges are pale golden.
Cool completely on a rack before storing.

title: Coconut Biscuits
description: A practical home-style Coconut Biscuits recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Preheat the oven to 175°C (350°F) and line two trays.
Beat butter and sugar on medium speed for 3 minutes. Beat in the egg for 30 seconds.
Fold in flour, baking powder, salt and desiccated coconut until a soft dough forms.
Shape into equal balls, flatten slightly and leave 4 cm between them.
Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until golden at the edges, then cool for 10 minutes on the tray.

title: Karachi Fruit Cake
description: A practical home-style Karachi Fruit Cake recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Preheat the oven to 170°C (340°F). Grease and line a loaf or 20 cm cake pan.
Toss the dried fruit and nuts with 1 tablespoon flour so they remain suspended.
Beat butter and sugar on medium speed for 4 minutes until light. Add eggs one at a time, beating 30 seconds after each.
Whisk flour, baking powder, salt and spices. Fold into the batter in 3 additions, alternating with milk.
Fold in the fruit and nuts, transfer to the pan and level the top.
Bake for 45 to 55 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool 15 minutes in the pan, then transfer to a rack.

title: Plain Sponge Cake
description: A practical home-style Plain Sponge Cake recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Preheat the oven to 175°C (350°F). Grease and line a 23 cm round pan.
Beat butter and sugar on medium-high speed for 4 minutes until pale and fluffy.
Add eggs one at a time, beating 30 seconds after each, then beat in vanilla.
Whisk flour, baking powder and salt. Add in 3 additions on low speed, alternating with milk, and stop as soon as combined.
Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the top springs back and a skewer comes out clean.
Cool for 10 minutes in the pan, then turn onto a rack.

title: Cream Rolls
description: A practical home-style Cream Rolls recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Preheat the oven to 200°C (400°F). Lightly grease metal cream-horn moulds.
Cut cold puff pastry into 2 cm strips and wrap each strip around a mould with a slight overlap.
Brush with beaten egg, sprinkle lightly with sugar and bake for 15 to 18 minutes until puffed and deep golden.
Cool for 5 minutes, slide out the moulds and cool the shells completely.
Whip cold cream, powdered sugar and vanilla on medium-high speed for 2 to 3 minutes until stiff peaks form.
Pipe cream into both ends just before serving and keep chilled.

title: Patties
description: A practical home-style Patties recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 2 cups cooked mixed vegetables, finely chopped
steps: Preheat the oven to 200°C (400°F) and line a tray.
Heat oil and cook onion for 4 minutes. Add vegetables and spices and cook until dry; cool completely.
Roll cold puff pastry to about 3 mm thick and cut into equal rectangles.
Place filling on one half, brush the edges with egg, fold and press firmly with a fork.
Brush the tops with egg and cut a small steam vent.
Bake for 18 to 22 minutes until puffed and golden. Cool for 5 minutes before serving.

title: Chicken Patties
description: A practical home-style Chicken Patties recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Preheat the oven to 200°C (400°F) and line a tray.
Cook the chicken filling over medium heat until the chicken reaches 74°C (165°F) and the mixture is dry; cool completely.
Roll cold puff pastry to 3 mm thick and cut into equal pieces.
Add filling, brush edges with beaten egg, fold and seal with a fork.
Brush the tops with egg and bake for 18 to 22 minutes until deeply golden.
Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

title: Lassi
description: A practical home-style Lassi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Chill the liquid ingredients and serving glasses before starting.
Add the main liquid, fruit, yogurt, nuts, herbs, sugar and seasonings to a blender in that order.
Blend on low speed for 20 seconds, then on high speed for 45 to 60 seconds until completely smooth.
Taste and adjust sweetness, salt or acidity. Add chilled water or ice gradually to reach the desired consistency.
Strain only when a clear drink is desired, then pour into cold glasses and serve immediately.

licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.

title: Masala Dosa
description: A practical home-style Masala Dosa recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the rice and urad dal separately. Soak the rice with fenugreek and the dal in separate bowls for 4 to 6 hours.
Drain and grind the dal with small additions of water for 4 to 5 minutes until light and fluffy. Grind the rice for 3 to 4 minutes to a smooth but slightly grainy batter.

Combine both batters with clean hands for 2 minutes. The batter should pour in a thick ribbon. Cover loosely and ferment in a warm place for 8 to 12 hours until increased in volume and lightly bubbly.
Add salt and enough water to reach a flowing pancake-batter consistency. For masala dosa, prepare the potato filling separately and keep it hot.
Heat a cast-iron griddle over medium-high heat to about 200°C (390°F). Sprinkle with water; it should sizzle and evaporate immediately.
Pour about 1/3 cup batter in the centre and spread quickly into a thin circle. Drizzle 1 teaspoon oil around the edges and cook for 2 to 3 minutes until crisp and golden.
Add chutney or potato filling when required, fold and serve immediately. Cool the griddle briefly between dosas if the batter sticks while spreading.
